blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
If the first four sessions were about understanding who you are, this session is about understanding what principles you will live by. The five Core Values — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage — are not a motivational checklist or a ministry buzzword set. They are DVULI's carefully curated set of principles that are often ignored or misunderstood yet are essential building blocks for developing and sustaining a healthy professional and personal life. This session introduces all five together to give participants a comprehensive map before the territory is explored in depth across Sessions 6–8. The goal is not mastery but orientation: participants should leave this session able to define each Core Value, articulate its Biblical basis, and identify where it is most and least present in their current ministry life.

The theological foundation of this session is Ecclesiastes 3:1 — "There is a time for everything, and a season for every activity under the heavens" — combined with the psalmist's cry in Psalm 26:2–3: "Test me, Lord, and try me, examine my heart and my mind." These passages frame the Core Values not as human-crafted competencies but as biblically grounded principles that reflect how God expects His leaders to live.


ACCOUNTABILITY

Accountability means voluntarily submitting your decisions, actions, and outcomes to honest scrutiny — by God, by trusted peers, and by the people your leadership affects. The operative word is voluntarily. Accountability that is only extracted under pressure is compliance, not accountability. A leader who is accountable creates the structures and relationships that make honest feedback possible before a crisis demands it.

The common confusion here is between accountability and blame. Blame is retrospective and punitive; it asks who is at fault. Accountability is formative and relational; it asks what is true and what must change. Urban ministry leaders who conflate the two tend to avoid both — they neither hold others accountable nor invite accountability themselves, because the entire concept feels like an attack rather than a resource.

Biblically, accountability is grounded in Galatians 2:11, where Paul opposes Peter to his face because Peter's behavior was clearly wrong. This is not a scene of organizational hierarchy — Peter outranked Paul in many eyes. It is a scene of covenantal honesty between leaders who understood that truth-telling was an act of love, not aggression.

In practice: A community development director in a mid-sized city discovers that her program metrics have been reported optimistically to funders for two quarters running. The temptation is to quietly correct the numbers and move on. An accountable leader instead names the gap internally, traces how it happened, and decides whether the funders need to be informed. That decision is costly. It is also what distinguishes a leader who builds durable trust from one who manages appearances.

Quiz: Which of the following best defines Accountability as a Core Value? (a) Assigning consequences when team members fail to meet expectations (b) Voluntarily submitting decisions and outcomes to honest scrutiny before a crisis demands it (c) Reporting accurate data to external stakeholders (d) Holding others to the standards you set for the organization

Quiz: A pastor learns his elder board has been avoiding a difficult conversation with a long-tenured staff member whose performance has declined. His response is to schedule a structured, honest review meeting and invite the staff member to participate in assessing the situation. This response best illustrates which Core Value? (a) Leverage (b) Empowerment (c) Accountability (d) Balance


BALANCE

Balance is the intentional distribution of energy across the domains of life — spiritual, relational, physical, and vocational — so that a leader can sustain effectiveness over time rather than sprint to exhaustion. Balance is not equality of hours. A leader in a crisis season will spend more time on crisis. Balance is the ongoing, honest assessment of whether your current distribution of energy is sustainable and whether it reflects your actual priorities.

The common confusion is between Balance and busyness management. Busyness management is reactive — it asks how to get through this week. Balance is structural — it asks whether the way I am living is producing the person and the leader I intend to become. Many urban ministry leaders are skilled at busyness management and deeply unbalanced, because the two skills are not the same and the second requires more courage than the first.

Biblically, Balance is anchored in Mark 6:30–31, where Jesus, upon hearing that his disciples had been so busy they had not even eaten, says: "Come with me by yourselves to a quiet place and get some rest." Jesus does not say this when the ministry slows down. He says it in the middle of a period of intense demand. Rest and renewal are not the reward for completed work; they are a condition for continued work.

In practice: An executive director of a youth-serving nonprofit has not taken a full day off in eleven weeks. He frames this as devotion to the mission. His staff have begun to mirror his pace; three have called in sick in the same month. His board chair asks him directly: "If you disappeared for two weeks, what would break?" The inability to answer that question is not a sign of indispensability — it is a sign of imbalance. A leader practicing Balance builds the team, the systems, and the personal rhythms that make sustainable leadership possible.

Quiz: Balance as a Core Value is best described as which of the following? (a) Spending equal time on each domain of life each week (b) Intentionally distributing energy across life domains to sustain effectiveness over time (c) Delegating tasks so that no single person carries too much workload (d) Avoiding overcommitment by saying no to new responsibilities

Quiz: A ministry leader schedules a quarterly personal retreat day regardless of how demanding the calendar looks, and uses it to assess whether her current pace reflects her stated priorities. This practice most directly embodies which Core Value? (a) Interdependence (b) Balance (c) Accountability (d) Leverage


INTERDEPENDENCE

Interdependence is the recognition that effective leadership is fundamentally relational and that a leader's capacity is multiplied, not diminished, by genuine partnership with others. Interdependence is not the same as collaboration, which can be superficial. It is the willingness to be genuinely affected by others — to allow their strengths to compensate for your gaps, their perspective to correct your blind spots, and their investment to extend your reach.

The common confusion is between Interdependence and dependence. Dependence is the inability to function without others; it signals unresolved immaturity. Interdependence is the choice to function with others because you understand that no individual, however gifted, contains everything the mission requires. Urban leaders who have survived by being self-sufficient often find Interdependence the most threatening Core Value, because it requires admitting need without losing authority.

Biblically, Interdependence is drawn from 1 Corinthians 12, where Paul's body metaphor is unambiguous: "The eye cannot say to the hand, 'I don't need you.'" Every member is necessary; the parts that seem weaker are indispensable. The body does not function when strong members override rather than integrate the contributions of others.

In practice: A faith-based housing organization is planning its largest capital campaign in ten years. The executive director is the primary relationship holder with major donors. Rather than running the campaign through her own relationships, she maps the board's networks, assigns relationship ownership across the team, and creates a structure where the campaign succeeds because of distributed effort — not because of her heroics. When she travels for a family emergency during the final push, the campaign does not stall. That is Interdependence made structural.

Quiz: Interdependence differs from simple collaboration primarily because it involves which of the following? (a) A formal memorandum of understanding between partner organizations (b) Willingness to be genuinely affected by others and allow their strengths to compensate for your gaps (c) Dividing tasks among team members to improve efficiency (d) Building consensus before making major decisions

Quiz: A lead pastor resists forming a co-leadership structure because he believes shared authority will create confusion in the congregation. According to DVULI's Core Values, which value is most directly being avoided? (a) Accountability (b) Leverage (c) Empowerment (d) Interdependence


EMPOWERMENT

Empowerment is the intentional development of others' capacity to lead — giving people real authority, genuine responsibility, and the support they need to succeed, then stepping back far enough for their leadership to take root. Empowerment is not assignment. A leader who assigns tasks but retains all decision-making has delegated work, not authority. Empowerment requires the leader to risk outcomes on someone else's development.

The critical distinction is between Empowerment and delegation. Delegation transfers a task. Empowerment transfers ownership. A delegated task comes back to you when it is complete. An empowered leader takes the task, learns from it, builds capacity around it, and eventually stops needing you in that domain. Urban ministry leaders who are afraid of being outgrown or replaced tend to delegate and call it empowerment; the people around them stop growing and eventually leave.

Biblically, Empowerment runs through 2 Timothy 2:2 — "The things you have heard me say in the presence of many witnesses entrust to reliable people who will also be qualified to teach others." This is not a transaction. It is a four-generation chain: Paul to Timothy to reliable people to others. Each link requires the previous link to genuinely release what it has received.

In practice: A church's youth director identifies a 24-year-old volunteer who consistently demonstrates maturity and vision. Rather than using her as additional staff capacity, the youth director begins bringing her into strategic conversations, letting her run a pilot program with real budget authority, and publicly crediting her leadership to the senior pastor. Within eighteen months the volunteer is ready for a salaried role. That outcome required the youth director to believe that developing someone else's leadership was the work, not a detour from it.

Quiz: Which statement best captures the distinction between Empowerment and delegation? (a) Delegation is for routine tasks; Empowerment is for complex projects (b) Delegation transfers a task; Empowerment transfers ownership and genuine authority (c) Empowerment requires a formal mentorship agreement; delegation does not (d) Delegation is top-down; Empowerment involves peer-to-peer accountability

Quiz: A nonprofit director assigns a program manager to lead a community needs assessment but reviews and approves every interview question and every stakeholder communication before it goes out. This situation illustrates a gap in which Core Value? (a) Balance (b) Accountability (c) Empowerment (d) Interdependence


LEVERAGE

Leverage is the strategic use of existing assets — relationships, resources, platforms, knowledge, and influence — to produce outcomes disproportionate to the inputs invested. Leverage asks: what do I already have that is being underused, and where does a small, well-placed investment generate the greatest return for the mission? It is not about doing more. It is about deploying what exists more wisely.

The common confusion is between Leverage and efficiency. Efficiency is doing the same things with less waste. Leverage often involves doing different things — identifying where your unique position, network, or credibility creates a force multiplier that others cannot replicate. Many urban leaders work efficiently in spaces where their leverage is low while ignoring spaces where their leverage is high, simply because the high-leverage opportunities look less like conventional work.

Biblically, Leverage appears in the mustard seed parable and is crystallized in John 12:24: "Unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces many seeds." The logic of leverage is multiplication through strategic release — the seed must leave the hand to produce the harvest.

In practice: A pastor leads a congregation of 200 in a neighborhood with a significant unhoused population. He cannot build a shelter. But he has a relationship with the city councilwoman, a building with a commercial kitchen, and a congregation with 40 consistent volunteers. A local service organization has the expertise and the funding but lacks community trust and physical space. The pastor convenes the partnership, contributes what only he can contribute, and the result is a weekly meal program that serves 150 people and operates largely without him once it is running. He did not do more work. He identified where his assets intersected with the mission's greatest need.

Quiz: Leverage as a Core Value is most accurately described as which of the following? (a) Maximizing the number of people a leader directly manages (b) Reducing organizational expenses through process improvement (c) Strategically deploying existing assets to produce outcomes disproportionate to inputs invested (d) Using one's positional authority to accelerate decision-making

Quiz: A ministry leader is invited to join the board of a citywide coalition. She declines because board meetings conflict with her Tuesday staff meetings. A colleague suggests she is missing an opportunity for Leverage. The colleague's concern is best understood as which of the following? (a) The leader is not managing her time efficiently (b) The leader may be failing to deploy her relational influence where its strategic return would be highest (c) The leader should empower her staff to run Tuesday meetings without her (d) The leader lacks the Balance to take on additional commitments


THE AGENDA FOR CHANGE

The session's practical anchor is the Agenda for Change framework — the three-step process of facing the truth about current reality, committing to change for a better future, and taking charge of change — that participants will use repeatedly throughout the program to translate Core Value insights into concrete commitments. Participants complete their first Agenda for Change reflection in this session, identifying one Core Value where they see breakthrough potential and one where they see breakdown risk. This reflection will be retrieved and deepened in Sessions 6, 7, and 8.

Session 5

The Architecture of a Flourishing Leader: The Five Core Values

A comprehensive orientation to the DVULI Core Values — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage — and the Agenda for Change framework that transforms insight into committed action.

Maria has been in urban youth ministry for eleven years. She prays faithfully, loves her community fiercely, and works harder than anyone she knows. But something is quietly breaking down. Three key volunteers left in the past year — two of them burned out, one frustrated that she was never allowed to lead anything significant. Maria hasn't taken a real day off in four months. She makes every decision herself because, she tells herself, "no one else really understands the vision." In her most honest moments, at 11 p.m. after everyone has gone home, she wonders why she feels more isolated than ever despite being surrounded by people who care about her.

Maria is not failing at doing ministry. She is failing at being the kind of leader her ministry needs. She lacks not skill, but principles — specifically, the five Core Values that the DVULI program has identified as "often ignored or misunderstood, yet essential building blocks for developing and sustaining a healthy professional and personal life." This session is your first encounter with all five together. You will not master them today. But you will leave with a map — and with the Agenda for Change tools to begin the journey.

Why Five Values? Why These Five?

Leadership development programs are full of value lists. Most fail to stick not because the values are wrong, but because they are presented as independent items on a checklist rather than as an interconnected architecture. The DVULI Core Values — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage — are different. They were not selected arbitrarily. They were curated from decades of experience with urban ministry leaders as the precise principles most commonly neglected and most powerfully transformative when practiced together (DVULI, 2024).

Notice what the five values are not. They are not a set of spiritual disciplines like prayer or fasting, though they deeply support the spiritual life. They are not organizational competencies like strategic planning or financial management, though they undergird organizational health. They sit in a different category entirely — they are principles for being, the character infrastructure from which sustainable leadership action flows. As Peter Senge (1990) observed in his landmark work on learning organizations, the most powerful leverage points in any complex system are found not in behaviors but in the mental models and values that generate those behaviors. Change the inner architecture, and the outer actions follow.

Robert Greenleaf (1977), whose servant leadership framework anticipated the DVULI model by several decades, made a similar argument: the best test of a leader is not efficiency or output but whether those being led grow as persons — "healthier, wiser, freer, more autonomous." The five Core Values are the structural conditions for that kind of leadership. When Maria neglects Empowerment, her volunteers cannot grow. When she neglects Balance, she cannot sustain. When she neglects Accountability, she cannot see herself clearly. The architecture fails because the components are missing.

Think About It

Before reading further, pause and consider: of the five Core Values listed above, which one do you suspect is your greatest area of strength in personal life? Which one do you suspect is your greatest area of need in ministry life? Hold those intuitions lightly as you read — the radar self-assessment widget below will give you a more precise picture.

The Being vs. Doing Distinction: The Heart of the Core Values

The DVULI program makes a foundational claim that deserves to be stated plainly before exploring any individual value: being one's best is the necessary foundation for doing one's best (DVULI, 2024). This is the Being vs. Doing distinction, and it runs beneath every Core Value like a deep aquifer.

Most ministry leaders are trained to focus on doing — running programs, counseling youth, recruiting volunteers, writing grants. The doing is real and important. But Parker Palmer (2010) argues compellingly that leaders who are disconnected from a rich inner life eventually find that their outer actions hollow out. The separation of "role" from "soul" is, in his framing, the primary source of leadership breakdown. Leaders become performers of ministry rather than practitioners of it; and the difference, over time, is everything.

This is why the Core Values begin with Accountability — honest self-examination — rather than with a skill or strategy. And it is why Balance — attending to the whole person, not just the professional function — precedes the relational values of Interdependence and Empowerment. The sequence is intentional: you cannot give away what you have not cultivated. Goleman, Boyatzis, and McKee (2002) demonstrated in their research on emotional intelligence that leader self-awareness — knowing one's own values, strengths, and limits — is the foundational competency from which all other leadership effectiveness flows. The Core Values are, in essence, a curriculum for that self-awareness.

"Test me, Lord, and try me, examine my heart and my mind; for I have always been mindful of your unfailing love and have lived in reliance on your faithfulness." — Psalm 26:2–3 (NIV)

The psalmist's prayer frames the entire enterprise. Honest examination of heart and mind — not to condemn but to align — is the posture the Core Values require. The goal is not perfection. The goal is orientation: knowing where you are so you can move toward where God is calling you to be.

The Five Core Values: A Comprehensive Map

Core Value 1: Accountability

Definition: Accountability stresses the importance of honest introspection, the prayerful search for wisdom, and the development of Accountability relationships — relationships characterized by a mutual commitment to seek and tell the truth concerning each other's thoughts and actions (DVULI, 2024).

The Problem It Addresses: The DVULI framework names this problem with memorable precision — Blind I's — our failure to see ourselves clearly. The Arbinger Institute (2000) provides a rigorous account of why this failure is so universal: when leaders betray their own sense of what is right, they enter a self-justifying cycle of perception and behavior that makes honest self-appraisal nearly impossible. In their language, leaders go "In the Box" — and once inside, the box becomes invisible. Dotlich, Noel, and Walker (2004) reach a similar conclusion through the lens of leadership passages: leaders who lack robust accountability frameworks tend to repeat developmental failures across their careers because they cannot see those failures clearly enough to learn from them.

The Solution — Fresh Eyes: The antidote is not self-criticism but honest seeking — what DVULI calls "fresh eyes." This involves three interlocking practices: introspection (regular, structured self-examination), self-disclosure (the courage to reveal yourself honestly to trusted others), and feedback (the discipline to receive truth from others without defensiveness).

Biblical Basis — Galatians 2:11: The primary text is Galatians 2:11, where Paul recounts his confrontation with Peter at Antioch: "When Cephas came to Antioch, I opposed him to his face, because he stood condemned." Leith Anderson (2003) observes that this is one of the most important leadership texts in the New Testament precisely because it models what Accountability looks like when it is costly. Paul did not manage Peter's feelings; he told him the truth. This kind of courageous truth-telling between people who are genuinely committed to each other's growth is the standard the Core Value sets. It is reinforced by the psalmist's prayer in Psalm 26:2–3 — not "tell me I'm doing well" but "test me, try me, examine my heart."

Key Indicator Questions:

  • Do I own the decisions I make and the actions I take, or do I make excuses and deceive myself?
  • Do I behave with integrity and act based on my mission and values, or am I more focused on my image and how others perceive me?
  • Do I heed those who hold me accountable, or do I offer excuses and resist their feedback? (DVULI, 2024)

Core Value 2: Balance

Definition: Balance stresses the importance of living in a way that pays attention to a balanced set of priorities, reflecting a commitment to wholeness versus busyness (DVULI, 2024).

The Problem It Addresses: DVULI names this problem boiling dry — the slow, invisible depletion that occurs when leaders give continuously without replenishment. Goleman, Boyatzis, and McKee (2002) call this the "sacrifice syndrome": leaders who pour themselves out in service to others, especially in high-demand environments like urban ministry, eventually reach a state of emotional and physical exhaustion that paradoxically makes them less effective at the very service they care about. Maria, in our opening scenario, is boiling dry. She does not need to work harder; she needs to stop equating busyness with faithfulness.

The Solution — Living by Principles: DVULI is careful to note that Balance is not a destination but a journey — "a lifelong process, not a destination" (DVULI, 2024). The solution is not a perfect schedule but a principle-centered life: one that deliberately attends to spiritual, physical, intellectual, emotional, and social dimensions of personhood because Jesus taught that we are whole persons, not just professional functions.

Biblical Basis — Mark 6:30–31: The text is deceptively simple: "The apostles gathered around Jesus and reported to him all they had done and taught. Then, because so many people were coming and going that they did not even have a chance to eat, he said to them, 'Come with me by yourselves to a quiet place and get some rest.'" Anderson (2003) notes that this passage appears precisely at a moment of maximum ministry intensity — the disciples had just returned from a remarkable mission trip. Jesus's response to success and momentum was not "press harder" but "come away and rest." The implications for leaders who never pause are sobering: if the Son of God built restorative rhythms into His ministry, there is no theological basis for leaders who do not.

Key Indicator Questions:

  • Do I focus on the important things based on my personal mission and values, or do I clutter my day with activity and busyness?
  • Do I see the connections between all aspects of my life, or do I compartmentalize?
  • Do I deliberately choose to organize my activities in a way that balances my life? (DVULI, 2024)
Think About It

Recall the last time you deliberately chose rest, renewal, or recreation in the face of mounting ministry demands. What made that choice easy or difficult? What did the choice (or its absence) reveal about your current relationship with Balance?

Core Value 3: Interdependence

Definition: Interdependence stresses the importance of living in community, acknowledging and relying on all of God's people, and collaborating effectively with the community's talented and committed members (DVULI, 2024).

The Problem It Addresses: Dancing solo is the DVULI diagnosis — the isolation that comes when leaders, often out of a combination of self-reliance, distrust, and the particular pressures of urban ministry, operate as solitary units rather than as members of a body. Senge (1990) describes this as a failure of systems thinking: the solo operator sees only the pieces they can directly touch and misses the interconnected whole. Palmer (2010) frames it ontologically: "We are in each other." The very nature of human beings as image-bearers of a Trinitarian God is relational, and leadership that denies that nature will, sooner or later, suffer for it.

Biblical Basis — 1 Corinthians 12: No text in Scripture articulates Interdependence more comprehensively than Paul's body metaphor: "The eye cannot say to the hand, 'I don't need you!' And the head cannot say to the feet, 'I don't need you!'" (1 Cor. 12:21). Every part is indispensable. Every part belongs. The parts that seem weaker are, in fact, indispensable. Anderson (2003) observes that Jesus modeled this value by sending disciples in pairs, forming the Twelve as a team, and never doing ministry in permanent isolation. The body image is not merely metaphorical: it describes an actual structure of mutual necessity. When any member operates independently of the others, the whole body suffers.

Key Indicator Questions:

  • Is the power being exercised by me and others in my partnerships being used for mutual good or for control?
  • Do we work together from our hearts or compete based on external factors?
  • Do I put the partnership first or put myself first? (DVULI, 2024)

Core Value 4: Empowerment

Definition: Empowerment stresses the importance of creating "leader-full" organizations by exercising servant leadership, allowing others to assume leadership, and developing skills in those around you (DVULI, 2024).

The Problem It Addresses: The vivid DVULI label is Action Heroes — leaders who are so capable, so driven, and so central to every decision that they become bottlenecks. The action hero leader is often well-intentioned: they genuinely believe they can do it better themselves. But Blanchard, Carlos, and Randolph (1996) demonstrate that this belief, however true in the short term, produces devastating long-term consequences: organizations where staff and volunteers serve the leader rather than the leader serving them; where capacity stagnates because no one else is developed; where succession is impossible because leadership is concentrated in one person.

The Solution — Leader-Full Organizations: Blanchard et al. (1996) describe empowerment not as delegation (giving tasks away) but as genuine investment in others' capacity to lead themselves. The key shift is from a controlling mindset to an equipping mindset — from "how do I get this done?" to "how do I develop the person God has placed before me?" The process DVULI identifies is: Identifying → Equipping → Mobilizing → Supporting. Each phase requires the leader to step back so that others can step forward.

Biblical Basis — 2 Timothy 2:2: "And the things you have heard me say in the presence of many witnesses entrust to reliable people who will also be qualified to teach others." This is the discipleship chain — four generations of investment in a single verse. Paul to Timothy. Timothy to reliable people. Reliable people to others. Greenleaf (1977) recognized this pattern as the fundamental mechanism of servant leadership: the leader's primary goal is not to accomplish a task but to increase the capacity of others to accomplish far more. Maria's ministry is constrained not by lack of talent in her volunteers but by her failure to invest in that talent.

Key Indicator Questions:

  • What do I call "mine" versus "ours" — my ministry versus our ministry, my will versus God's will?
  • Do I focus on growing others and increasing the capacity of my ministry, or on using others to fill specific tasks?
  • Do my staff and volunteers serve me, or do I serve them? (DVULI, 2024)

Core Value 5: Leverage

Definition: Leverage stresses the importance of looking for ways in which seemingly small actions can have a powerful and lasting impact for the Kingdom (DVULI, 2024).

The Problem It Addresses: Quick fixes that create long-lasting failures. Many leaders in high-need environments are reactive by necessity — there is always a crisis that demands immediate attention. But Senge's (1990) concept of leverage points illuminates why reactive leadership perpetuates the very problems it addresses: interventions at the symptom level (the quick fix) often create compensating feedback loops that make the underlying problem worse over time. The leader who stays up every night to cover a gap in volunteers is not leveraging; they are depleting. The leader who invests three months in developing a volunteer coordinator is leveraging — a small, patient investment that releases sustained capacity.

Biblical Basis — John 12:24 and the Mustard Seed: "Very truly I tell you, unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces many seeds." The paradox of Leverage is embedded in the paradox of death and resurrection: what looks like loss is actually the mechanism of multiplication. Jesus's mustard seed parable in Matthew 13:31–32 makes the same point from a different angle — not loss but patient smallness, the willingness to be insignificant in the short term for the sake of disproportionate impact over time. Senge (1990) identified "leverage points" in complex systems as exactly this: places where small interventions produce large results, if only the leader has the patience and perspective to find them.

Key Indicator Questions:

  • Do I focus proactively on the big picture or get distracted reacting to events?
  • Am I bold in making an impact, or do I hide out of fear?
  • Do I actively seek choices that have a disproportionately large impact, or just do what comes along? (DVULI, 2024)

Interactive Exploration: Core Values Radar Self-Assessment

The following widget invites you to assess yourself across three dimensions for each Core Value: (1) how well you understand it, (2) how consistently you apply it in your personal life, and (3) how consistently you apply it in your ministry. Use the sliders to set your ratings (1 = very low, 5 = excellent). You can also set an ideal profile at the beginning to see the distance between aspiration and current reality. Areas where personal and ministry application differ by two or more points are flagged in amber.

The Core Values as an Interconnected System

One of the most important insights in the DVULI framework is that the five Core Values are not independent items on a checklist — they are an interconnected system in which each value amplifies the others. Senge's (1990) systems thinking framework helps explain why: in any complex adaptive system, the components are so interrelated that changing one component inevitably affects all the others. The five Core Values exhibit exactly this kind of systemic interdependence.

Consider how the values reinforce each other in practice. Accountability — honest self-examination — generates the self-knowledge needed to practice Balance well. You cannot calibrate your rhythms of work and rest accurately if you cannot see yourself honestly. Balance, in turn, creates the reservoir of energy and perspective needed to practice Interdependence: a depleted leader cannot be truly present in community. Interdependence — living genuinely in relationship with others — provides the relational soil in which Empowerment can grow: you can only pour into others when you are not isolated. And Empowerment — releasing others into their calling — is the mechanism by which Leverage becomes possible: the multiplied impact of many empowered leaders is, by definition, greater than the impact of one depleted hero acting alone.

Equally important is how the failure of one value cascades through the system. Maria's ministry illustrates this precisely. Her failure at Empowerment means she is not practicing Interdependence with her volunteers at the level of leadership. Her failure at Balance means her failure at Empowerment is invisible to her — she is too busy doing everything herself to notice that she is not developing anyone. And her failure at Accountability — her unwillingness to seek honest feedback about her leadership patterns — means none of this is being surfaced and addressed. Restore Accountability, and the entire system becomes potentially self-correcting. This is leverage in its most profound form.

"There is a time for everything, and a season for every activity under the heavens." — Ecclesiastes 3:1 (NIV)

This verse, which anchors the Core Values framework theologically, is not simply a meditation on the passage of time. It is a statement about design — about the wisdom embedded in creation that assigns different activities to different seasons. The Core Values are, in essence, an invitation to live in alignment with that design: to give each dimension of leadership life its proper season rather than crowding everything into the single, relentless season of activity. As Anderson (2003) observes, Jesus modeled this seasonal intelligence with remarkable consistency — seasons of teaching and seasons of withdrawal, seasons of collective ministry and seasons of solitary prayer, seasons of intensive investment in the Twelve and seasons of broad public proclamation.

Discovering Your Biblical Foundations

The five Core Values are not merely pragmatic principles that happen to align with Scripture — they are grounded in Scripture, reflecting how God expects His leaders to live (DVULI, 2024). Each value has a primary biblical anchor text, and understanding that text at depth is part of what it means to make the value genuinely your own rather than simply adopting its language.

The following matching game invites you to connect each Core Value to its primary biblical foundation. The exercise is not merely trivia; it is a form of retrieval practice that encodes the theological roots more deeply. As you play, pay attention to the brief explanations offered when a correct match is made — those summaries represent the core of what each text is asking of you as a leader.

Biblical Foundation Matching Game
Click a Core Value card, then click its matching Scripture card. Correct matches reveal the theological connection.
Score: 0 / 5  |  Attempts: 0

The Agenda for Change: Translating Insight into Committed Action

Understanding the Core Values intellectually is the beginning, not the end. The DVULI program is explicit: the goal is not mastery but Breakthrough — "strategic, significant, and sustainable change in the way you live and work" (DVULI, 2024). The mechanism for translating Core Values insight into that kind of change is the Agenda for Change — a three-step framework that you will use repeatedly across Sessions 6, 7, and 8 as each Core Value is explored in depth.

Step 1: Facing the Truth About Current Reality

The first step is the most commonly skipped and the most important. The DVULI workbook is direct: "We are often in such a hurry to move into action that we fail to see the whole story of our current reality. We need to let the truth 'sink in' by reflecting on our current reality, connecting to what we are learning and acknowledging the impact of our current actions" (DVULI, 2024).

The Arbinger Institute (2000) provides the theoretical framework for why this step is both essential and difficult: leaders who are caught in self-justifying narratives — who are, in Arbinger's language, "In the Box" — cannot see their current reality accurately. Their perception is filtered through the need to maintain a favorable self-image. Genuinely facing the truth requires, first, the willingness to step outside that self-justifying bubble — to let the honest observations of trusted others, the evidence of your own journal, and the probing of the Key Indicator Questions disrupt comfortable self-assessments.

Step 2: Committing to Change for a Better Future

"Knowing what we should do and committing to doing it are not the same thing" (DVULI, 2024). This step bridges insight and intention. It involves three movements: first, discovering specific opportunities for healthy change — naming, as precisely as possible, what needs to shift; second, honestly reckoning with the cost of not making those changes (relationship damage? burnout? ministry stagnation?); and third, envisioning what a different reality would actually look like.

Dotlich, Noel, and Walker (2004) observe that leaders who have experienced significant developmental "crucibles" — painful seasons of failure or transition — only extract formative learning if they engage in genuine reflective commitment. The crucible experience without the commitment to change produces bitterness or denial. The commitment without the honest facing of reality produces superficial behavioral adjustment that does not last. It is the combination — truth plus commitment — that Dotlich et al. call the foundation of mature leadership.

Step 3: Taking Charge of Change

"Deciding to change doesn't automatically make it so" (DVULI, 2024). The third step is the translation of commitment into concrete, accountable action. It requires specificity: not "I will work on Balance" but "I will block two hours every Saturday morning for personal renewal, beginning this weekend." It requires a deadline. And it requires an accountability structure — a mentor, a partner, or a city group peer who will ask the hard follow-up question: "Did you do what you said?"

Your Core Values mentor plays a vital role at this stage. Fred Smith's classic guidance on mentoring (referenced in the DVULI Pre-Assignment) is apt: "Accountability is like a tail on the kite — it keeps things from darting around" (Smith, 1999). The third step is where the Agenda for Change becomes most vulnerable to abandonment, and where the relational infrastructure of the DVULI community — mentor, city group, facilitator — becomes most essential.

Think About It

As you prepare to work through the widget below, consider: which of the five Core Values, if genuinely developed in your life, would produce the greatest breakthrough — strategic, significant, and sustainable change — in how you lead? Which Core Value, if neglected, poses the greatest breakdown risk? These two questions will anchor your first Agenda for Change reflection.

Your First Agenda for Change Reflection

Complete the three-panel Agenda for Change worksheet below, focusing on one Core Value where you sense either breakthrough potential or breakdown risk. This reflection will be retrieved and deepened in Sessions 6, 7, and 8. Take your time — Step 1 in particular deserves more thought than most leaders initially give it.

Agenda for Change — Interactive Worksheet
Complete all three panels. A summary card will appear after Step 3 for you to save or revisit in Sessions 6–8.
Step 1 — Facing the Truth About Current Reality
No severity selected yet.
Step 2 — Committing to Change for a Better Future
Step 3 — Taking Charge of Change
Your Agenda for Change — Summary Card

📌 Take a screenshot or copy this summary. You will retrieve it in Sessions 6, 7, and 8 as each Core Value is explored in depth.

The Orientation You Now Carry

This session's goal, as stated at the outset, was not mastery but orientation — a comprehensive map before the territory is explored in depth. You should now be able to define each Core Value in your own words, articulate its primary biblical anchor, and identify where it is most and least present in your current ministry life.

Carry two things forward from this session. First, the systemic insight: the five values are not five separate commitments but one interlocking architecture. Neglect any one of them and the others will compensate imperfectly for a while, then begin to fail. Strengthen any one of them and the others will benefit from the ripple effects. This is the kind of thinking that Senge (1990) calls "systems thinking" and that the men of Issachar — those who "understood the times and knew what Israel should do" (1 Chronicles 12:32, NIV) — practiced instinctively in a different age.

Second, carry the Agenda for Change itself. The three-step process — Facing the Truth, Committing to Change, Taking Charge — is not a one-time exercise. It is a repeatable, portable technology for personal and professional transformation that you will apply to each Core Value in Sessions 6–8. The reflection you completed in the widget above is your first application. Protect it. Revisit it. When you return to it in later sessions, you will have both the depth of additional content and the distance of time to see it with fresh eyes — which is, fittingly, the most important gift that Accountability can give.

Key Takeaways

  • The five Core Values — Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage — are "essential building blocks for developing and sustaining a healthy professional and personal life," identified by DVULI as principles that are "often ignored or misunderstood" yet powerfully transformative when practiced together.
  • The Being vs. Doing distinction is foundational: being one's best is the necessary precondition for doing one's best. The Core Values are primarily principles for being, not techniques for doing.
  • Each Core Value has a specific biblical anchor: Accountability (Galatians 2:11), Balance (Mark 6:30–31), Interdependence (1 Corinthians 12), Empowerment (2 Timothy 2:2), and Leverage (John 12:24).
  • The Core Values function as an interconnected system: failure in one cascades into the others; growth in one creates ripple effects across the whole architecture.
  • The Agenda for Change — Facing the Truth, Committing to Change, Taking Charge — is the three-step framework that translates Core Values insight into strategic, significant, and sustainable change.
  • Your first Agenda for Change reflection (completed today) focuses on one Core Value where you identify breakthrough potential or breakdown risk, and will be retrieved and deepened in Sessions 6, 7, and 8.

Looking Ahead

Session 6 takes the first Core Value — Accountability — from orientation into depth. You will explore the anatomy of self-deception, the practice of building and sustaining Accountability relationships, and the specific tools DVULI uses for honest peer feedback. Come prepared with your Agenda for Change reflection from this session: if Accountability was your breakthrough or breakdown focus today, you will extend that work considerably. If not, Session 6 will challenge you to reconsider whether your current rating was as honest as it needed to be.

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: Five Core Values defined: Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, Leverage — their Biblical roots and ministry applications
Agenda for Change: three-step framework for translating insight into committed action (Facing Truth / Committing to Change / Taking Charge)
Being vs. Doing: the Core Values underscore that being one's best is the necessary foundation for doing one's best
The Core Values as interconnected system: how each value amplifies the others

items.question: Which of the following correctly lists all five Core Values introduced in this session?
items.correctFeedback: The DVULI's five Core Values are Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage. These are described as carefully curated principles that are 'essential building blocks for developing and sustaining a healthy professional and personal life.'

distractors.text: Accountability, Balance, Independence, Empowerment, Leverage
distractors.feedback: Students may confuse Interdependence with Independence. The Core Value is specifically Interdependence — mutual reliance within the body of Christ — which is fundamentally different from Independence, which implies self-sufficiency.

distractors.text: Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Encouragement, Leverage
distractors.feedback: Encouragement is a valuable ministry practice but is not one of the five Core Values. The correct term is Empowerment, which focuses on developing others to lead, not simply encouraging them.

distractors.text: Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, Legacy
distractors.feedback: Legacy may sound fitting for a leadership program, but the fifth Core Value is Leverage — the principle of multiplying impact through strategic investment, rooted in imagery like the mustard seed parable.

items.question: According to the session, the five Core Values are best described as principles that are which of the following?
items.correctAnswer: Often ignored or misunderstood, yet essential for a healthy professional and personal life
items.correctFeedback: The session explicitly characterizes the Core Values as principles that 'are often ignored or misunderstood' yet are 'essential building blocks for developing and sustaining a healthy professional and personal life.' This framing emphasizes that they are countercultural and easily overlooked.

distractors.text: Widely practiced in ministry settings but rarely given formal names
distractors.feedback: This reverses the session's characterization. The Core Values are described as often ignored or misunderstood — not widely practiced. The problem the session addresses is that leaders frequently overlook these principles.

distractors.text: Advanced leadership competencies for experienced senior pastors
distractors.feedback: The session frames the Core Values as foundational building blocks for all leaders, not advanced competencies reserved for senior leaders. They are described as essential for anyone developing a healthy professional and personal life.

distractors.text: Motivational ideals that inspire leaders to strive for excellence
distractors.feedback: The session explicitly warns against viewing the Core Values as a 'motivational checklist or a ministry buzzword set.' They are grounded, practical principles — not aspirational ideals meant to inspire.

items.question: Which biblical passage serves as the foundation for the Core Value of Interdependence?
items.correctAnswer: 1 Corinthians 12 and its metaphor of the body of Christ
items.correctFeedback: Interdependence is grounded in Paul's body metaphor in 1 Corinthians 12, which teaches that each member of the body has a distinct function and that no part can say to another, 'I don't need you.' This passage powerfully illustrates mutual reliance within the community of faith.

distractors.text: Galatians 2:11 and Paul's confrontation of Peter
distractors.feedback: Galatians 2:11 is the biblical foundation for Accountability, not Interdependence. Paul opposing Peter 'to his face' illustrates courageous truth-telling, which is central to holding one another accountable.

distractors.text: Mark 6:30–31 and Jesus' invitation to rest
distractors.feedback: Mark 6:30–31 ('Come with me by yourselves to a quiet place and get some rest') is the biblical foundation for Balance, not Interdependence. It reflects the restorative rhythm Jesus modeled for his disciples.

distractors.text: 2 Timothy 2:2 and the discipleship chain
distractors.feedback: 2 Timothy 2:2 is the biblical foundation for Empowerment, not Interdependence. That passage describes entrusting truth to reliable people who will teach others — a chain of discipleship and development.

items.question: What are the three steps of the Agenda for Change framework in their correct order?
items.correctAnswer: Facing the Truth About Current Reality, Committing to Change for a Better Future, Taking Charge of Change
items.correctFeedback: The Agenda for Change follows a deliberate sequence: first honestly assessing where you are (Facing Truth), then making a deliberate decision to pursue something different (Committing to Change), and finally taking ownership of the process (Taking Charge). This order reflects the logic that action without honest assessment leads to misdirected effort.

distractors.text: Committing to Change for a Better Future, Facing the Truth About Current Reality, Taking Charge of Change
distractors.feedback: This places commitment before honest assessment. The framework intentionally begins with Facing Truth because committing to change without first understanding current reality leads to uninformed or misdirected commitments.

distractors.text: Taking Charge of Change, Facing the Truth About Current Reality, Committing to Change for a Better Future
distractors.feedback: This reverses the framework's logic by placing action first. The Agenda for Change is built on the principle that sustainable change begins with honest assessment, not premature action.

distractors.text: Facing the Truth About Current Reality, Taking Charge of Change, Committing to Change for a Better Future
distractors.feedback: While this correctly places Facing Truth first, it reverses the final two steps. In the framework, commitment must precede action — a leader must decide to change before they can take charge of the change process.

items.question: The Core Value of Leverage is biblically grounded in the mustard seed parable and John 12:24. What principle does this grounding most directly illustrate?
items.correctAnswer: Small, sacrificial investments can produce disproportionately large and multiplied impact
items.correctFeedback: Both the mustard seed parable and John 12:24 ('Unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed') illustrate how something small or seemingly lost can generate outsized results. Leverage as a Core Value captures this principle of strategic, sacrificial investment that multiplies impact far beyond its original scope.

distractors.text: Leaders should use their positional authority to move others toward organizational goals
distractors.feedback: This reflects a secular, power-based understanding of leverage. The biblical grounding in the mustard seed and dying grain points to sacrificial investment and multiplication, not the use of positional authority to direct others.

distractors.text: God rewards faithfulness with increasingly larger ministry platforms
distractors.feedback: While stewardship and faithfulness are biblical themes, this answer focuses on personal reward and platform growth. The Core Value of Leverage is about multiplying impact through strategic, often sacrificial action — not about receiving larger platforms.

distractors.text: Ministry leaders should focus their energy on the single most impactful program available
distractors.feedback: This reduces Leverage to a time-management tactic. The biblical imagery of seeds dying and growing points to a deeper principle about sacrificial investment producing multiplication, not merely about choosing the highest-impact program.

items.question: During the first Agenda for Change reflection in this session, participants are asked to identify which two things?
items.correctAnswer: One Core Value where they see breakthrough potential and one where they see breakdown risk
items.correctFeedback: The session's practical anchor asks participants to honestly assess which Core Value represents their greatest opportunity for growth (breakthrough potential) and which represents their greatest vulnerability (breakdown risk). This dual reflection mirrors the Facing Truth step of the Agenda for Change framework.

distractors.text: Their strongest Core Value and the biblical passage that supports it
distractors.feedback: While biblical foundations are discussed in the session, the Agenda for Change reflection is not about matching values to passages. It focuses on identifying both an area of promise and an area of vulnerability in one's current ministry life.

distractors.text: A personal mission statement and a ministry action plan for the next quarter
distractors.feedback: This answer describes a strategic planning exercise, not the Agenda for Change reflection. The session's reflection is more diagnostic — identifying where a leader is strong and where they are at risk — not prescriptive at this stage.

distractors.text: Two Core Values they plan to master before the next session
distractors.feedback: The session explicitly states that 'the goal is not mastery but orientation.' Participants are not expected to master any Core Value at this point; they are beginning a process of honest assessment that will deepen across Sessions 6 through 8.

items.question: How does the session describe the relationship between 'being' and 'doing' in the context of the five Core Values?
items.correctAnswer: Being one's best is the necessary foundation for doing one's best
items.correctFeedback: The Core Values underscore that character and internal health (being) must precede effective action (doing). This principle reflects the session's emphasis that who a leader is determines the quality and sustainability of what a leader does.

distractors.text: Doing faithful ministry work will eventually transform who a leader is on the inside
distractors.feedback: This reverses the session's logic. The Core Values teach that being shapes doing — not the other way around. The session positions internal health as the prerequisite for, not the result of, external ministry effectiveness.

distractors.text: Being and doing are equally important and should receive identical attention from leaders
distractors.feedback: While both being and doing matter, the session does not frame them as co-equal. It specifically teaches that being is the necessary foundation — it has logical and developmental priority over doing.

distractors.text: Leaders should focus exclusively on being, trusting God to handle the doing
distractors.feedback: The session does not advocate abandoning action. It teaches that being is the foundation for doing — both are part of the leader's responsibility, but internal health must come first to sustain effective external action.

items.question: A youth ministry leader notices she always takes on every task herself, struggles to delegate, and feels exhausted. Using the Core Values framework, which TWO values most directly address her situation?
items.correctFeedback: Empowerment addresses her failure to develop and release others to lead (the 2 Timothy 2:2 principle of entrusting responsibilities to reliable people). Balance addresses her exhaustion and lack of restorative rhythm, reflecting Jesus' model in Mark 6:30–31. Both values speak directly to the symptoms she is experiencing.

distractors.text: Accountability and Leverage
distractors.feedback: While Accountability might help this leader receive honest feedback about her patterns, it does not directly address her inability to delegate or her exhaustion. Leverage concerns multiplying impact strategically, which is related but less directly relevant than Empowerment, which specifically involves developing others to lead.

distractors.text: Interdependence and Accountability
distractors.feedback: Interdependence — recognizing mutual reliance — is somewhat relevant but does not specifically address delegation and developing others, which is the domain of Empowerment. Accountability could help but does not directly address her exhaustion, which is a Balance issue.

distractors.text: Leverage and Interdependence
distractors.feedback: Leverage involves multiplying impact through strategic investment, and Interdependence involves mutual reliance, but neither directly targets her specific problems of failing to develop others (Empowerment) and neglecting personal rest and renewal (Balance).

items.question: Why does the session present all five Core Values together before exploring each one in depth across Sessions 6 through 8?
items.correctAnswer: To provide a comprehensive orientation map so participants can see how the values form an interconnected system before studying each in detail
items.correctFeedback: The session's stated goal is 'not mastery but orientation.' By introducing all five values together, participants gain a comprehensive view of how they interrelate as a system before diving deeper. This reflects the principle that the Core Values are interconnected — each amplifies the others — making a holistic overview essential before detailed study.

distractors.text: To allow participants to choose which Core Values they want to focus on and skip the rest
distractors.feedback: The Core Values are presented as an interconnected system in which each value amplifies the others. The session does not present them as a menu from which leaders select favorites; all five are treated as essential building blocks.

distractors.text: To ensure participants achieve full mastery of all five values before moving to application exercises, building confidence through cumulative learning
distractors.feedback: The session explicitly states that 'the goal is not mastery but orientation.' Full mastery is not expected at this stage; the overview is designed to provide a framework that will be deepened through subsequent sessions.

distractors.text: To rank the Core Values in order of importance so participants know which to prioritize first
distractors.feedback: The session does not rank the values hierarchically. As an interconnected system, each Core Value amplifies the others, and the overview is designed to show their relationships — not to establish a priority sequence.

items.question: A pastor uses the Agenda for Change framework and identifies that he avoids honest feedback from his team (Facing Truth), decides he needs regular accountability conversations (Committing to Change), and schedules monthly one-on-one meetings with a trusted peer (Taking Charge). Which Core Value is he most directly applying, and why does the sequential structure of the framework matter?
items.correctAnswer: Accountability; the sequential structure ensures that honest self-assessment precedes commitment, which precedes action, preventing superficial or unsustainable change
items.correctFeedback: The pastor's situation — avoiding feedback and then creating structures for truth-telling — directly reflects the Core Value of Accountability, grounded in the courageous honesty of Galatians 2:11. The Agenda for Change's three-step sequence matters because each step builds on the previous one: without honest diagnosis, commitment is uninformed; without genuine commitment, action lacks staying power. This produces sustainable rather than superficial change.

distractors.text: Interdependence; the sequential structure matters because it forces leaders to involve others before taking any action, ensuring collaborative decision-making at each stage
distractors.feedback: While scheduling meetings involves another person, the pastor's core issue is avoidance of honest feedback — an Accountability problem, not primarily an Interdependence issue. Additionally, the framework's sequence is about moving from truth to commitment to action, not about requiring collaboration at each step.

distractors.text: Empowerment; the sequential structure matters because it develops the other person's leadership capacity through the mentoring relationship
distractors.feedback: Empowerment involves developing others to lead (the 2 Timothy 2:2 chain). The pastor's goal is to receive honest feedback about himself, not to develop his peer's leadership. The framework's value lies in structuring personal change, not in its effect on others.

distractors.text: Balance; the sequential structure matters because it prevents leaders from overcommitting to too many changes at once
distractors.feedback: The pastor's issue is not overwork or lack of rest but avoidance of honest feedback, which is an Accountability issue. While the framework does promote thoughtful change, its sequential logic is about ensuring truth precedes commitment and commitment precedes action — not about limiting the number of changes.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the five Core Values introduced in Session 5?
options: Resilience
Accountability
Interdependence
Leverage

feedback.correct: The five Core Values are Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage. Resilience, while a positive trait, is not one of the DVULI's five Core Values. These five were specifically curated as 'essential building blocks for developing and sustaining a healthy professional and personal life.'
feedback.incorrect: Review the five Core Values carefully: Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage. Each was deliberately selected by DVULI and paired with a specific biblical foundation. Make sure you can distinguish these from other positive leadership qualities that are not part of this specific framework.

variants.stem: Which set correctly lists ALL five Core Values introduced in Session 5?
options: Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, Leverage
Accountability, Balance, Integrity, Empowerment, Leverage
Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Excellence, Leverage
Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, Legacy

feedback.correct: The five Core Values are Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage. Notice that terms like Integrity, Excellence, and Legacy — while admirable — are not part of this specific framework. The DVULI describes these five as principles that 'are often ignored or misunderstood.'
feedback.incorrect: Several of the options include words that sound like plausible leadership values but are not part of the DVULI framework. The correct five are Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage. Each has a specific biblical foundation introduced in this session.

questions.stem: Which biblical passage is cited as the foundation for the Core Value of Accountability?
options: Galatians 2:11 — Paul opposing Peter to his face
Mark 6:30–31 — Jesus inviting disciples to rest
1 Corinthians 12 — the body of Christ metaphor
2 Timothy 2:2 — the discipleship chain

feedback.correct: Accountability is grounded in Galatians 2:11, where Paul confronts Peter publicly about his hypocrisy. This passage illustrates courageous truth-telling — the willingness to hold one another to a standard even when the conversation is uncomfortable. This is the essence of accountability among leaders.
feedback.incorrect: Each Core Value is paired with a specific biblical passage: Accountability with Galatians 2:11 (Paul opposing Peter), Balance with Mark 6:30–31 (rest), Interdependence with 1 Corinthians 12 (body metaphor), Empowerment with 2 Timothy 2:2 (discipleship chain), and Leverage with John 12:24 (the kernel of wheat). Review these pairings carefully.

variants.stem: The Core Value of Empowerment is biblically rooted in which passage?
options: 2 Timothy 2:2 — entrusting truth to reliable people who can teach others
Galatians 2:11 — Paul confronting Peter about hypocrisy
John 12:24 — unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies
Ecclesiastes 3:1 — a time for everything under the heavens

feedback.correct: Empowerment is anchored in 2 Timothy 2:2, where Paul instructs Timothy to entrust what he has learned to reliable people who will also be qualified to teach others. This passage captures the discipleship chain — the multiplicative power of investing in others rather than hoarding influence.
feedback.incorrect: Each Core Value has a specific biblical pairing. Empowerment uses 2 Timothy 2:2 (the discipleship chain), not one of the other passages. John 12:24 (the kernel of wheat) is paired with Leverage, and Galatians 2:11 with Accountability. Make sure you can match each value to its scripture.

variants.stem: Which biblical text anchors the Core Value of Balance?
options: Mark 6:30–31 — Jesus telling the disciples to come away and rest
1 Corinthians 12 — many parts forming one body
Galatians 2:11 — Paul rebuking Peter publicly
John 12:24 — the kernel of wheat that must die to bear fruit

feedback.correct: Balance is grounded in Mark 6:30–31, where Jesus models restorative rhythm by inviting his busy disciples to 'come with me by yourselves to a quiet place and get some rest.' This passage demonstrates that rest is not weakness — it is a leadership practice modeled by Jesus himself.
feedback.incorrect: Balance is paired with Mark 6:30–31, Jesus's invitation to rest. The other passages correspond to different Core Values: 1 Corinthians 12 to Interdependence, Galatians 2:11 to Accountability, and John 12:24 to Leverage.

questions.stem: A youth ministry leader realizes she has been avoiding a difficult conversation with a volunteer who consistently undermines team decisions. She decides to use the Agenda for Change framework. What should her FIRST step be?
options: Facing the Truth About Current Reality
Committing to Change for a Better Future
Taking Charge of Change
Identifying a Core Values mentor to guide the conversation
justifications: The Agenda for Change begins with honest assessment of the present situation before any commitments or action steps, because change that skips truth-telling is built on an unstable foundation.
The first step is always the hardest action step, so taking charge immediately demonstrates courage and accountability.
Facing the truth means confronting the other person first so that they understand the problem before you commit to change.
The three steps can be done in any order depending on the situation, but facing truth is the easiest to start with.

feedback.correct: The Agenda for Change follows a deliberate sequence: first Face the Truth about current reality, then Commit to Change for a better future, and finally Take Charge of that change. This order matters because lasting change requires an honest reckoning with where you actually are before you can meaningfully commit to where you want to go.
feedback.incorrect: The Agenda for Change is a three-step sequential framework, not a flexible menu. It begins with Facing the Truth About Current Reality — honest self-assessment — before moving to commitment and then action. Identifying a mentor is part of the broader program but not a step within this specific framework.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify 'Facing the Truth' as the first step but misunderstand what it involves. It is about honest self-assessment of the current situation — not about immediately confronting others. The framework also has a fixed sequence, not a flexible order.

variants.stem: A pastor recognizes that he has been doing everything himself and failing to develop other leaders. Using the Agenda for Change framework, what must he do AFTER honestly acknowledging this pattern but BEFORE taking concrete action steps?
options: Commit to Change for a Better Future
Face the Truth About Current Reality
Take Charge of Change
Select which Core Value applies to his situation
justifications: Between acknowledging reality and acting on it, the framework requires a deliberate commitment to a different future, bridging insight and action with intentional resolve.
Committing to change means publicly declaring your intentions to your accountability partner before you have a plan.
This step is about emotional readiness — you must feel fully prepared before moving to action.
Commitment in this framework refers to selecting the single Core Value that will guide all future decisions.

feedback.correct: After Facing the Truth (step 1), the leader must Commit to Change for a Better Future (step 2) before Taking Charge (step 3). This middle step is crucial — it bridges the gap between awareness and action with deliberate intentionality. Without commitment, insight fades into good intentions that never materialize.
feedback.incorrect: The Agenda for Change has three sequential steps: Face the Truth, Commit to Change, Take Charge. The question specifies the leader has already completed the first step (honest acknowledgment). The next step is Committing to Change — not jumping straight to action or going back to re-examine reality.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly identify 'Commit to Change' but misunderstand what it involves. It is about forming deliberate resolve for a different future — not about public declarations, emotional readiness, or selecting a single Core Value.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The five Core Values emphasize that 'being one's best' is the necessary foundation for 'doing one's best' in ministry leadership.
questions.reason: Ecclesiastes 3:1 teaches that there is a time for everything, establishing that leaders must prioritize seasons of inner formation before seasons of outward activity.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, but Ecclesiastes 3:1 does not directly explain the Being vs. Doing principle. Ecclesiastes 3:1 speaks to the rhythm and timing of all activities under heaven — it frames the Core Values as seasonally appropriate, not as a hierarchy of being over doing. The Being vs. Doing principle stands on its own theological and practical logic: character shapes conduct, and who you are determines the quality of what you produce.
feedback.incorrect: This is tricky because both statements are genuinely true and both appear in Session 5. However, the key question is whether Ecclesiastes 3:1 actually EXPLAINS why being must precede doing. Ecclesiastes 3:1 is about seasons and timing, not about the priority of character over activity. The Being vs. Doing principle is supported by other theological reasoning — that inner formation is the wellspring of effective outward action.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The Core Value of Interdependence teaches that leaders must rely on others rather than working in isolation.
variants.reason: In 1 Corinthians 12, Paul uses the metaphor of the body to show that every part is necessary and no single member can function alone.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the 1 Corinthians 12 body metaphor directly explains why Interdependence is a Core Value. Paul's argument that 'the eye cannot say to the hand, I don't need you' provides the theological basis for the claim that leaders must rely on others. The passage is explicitly cited as Interdependence's biblical foundation.
feedback.incorrect: Consider whether the body metaphor in 1 Corinthians 12 directly supports the claim about Interdependence. Paul's point in that passage is precisely that no member of the body can function independently — each part needs the others. This is the specific biblical text cited as the foundation for the Core Value of Interdependence.

questions.stem: The session describes the five Core Values as an 'interconnected system.' What does this mean in practice?
options: Each Core Value amplifies and reinforces the others, so weakness in one area undermines the effectiveness of the rest
The five Core Values must be developed in a fixed sequence, starting with Accountability and ending with Leverage
Each Core Value is independent and can be fully mastered without reference to the other four
The interconnected system means that a leader only needs to excel in two or three of the five values to be effective

feedback.correct: The Core Values function as an interconnected system where each value amplifies the others. For example, Accountability without Balance leads to burnout, and Empowerment without Interdependence creates isolated silos. The framework is not sequential or selective — it requires attention to all five values because they mutually reinforce healthy leadership.
feedback.incorrect: The interconnected nature of the Core Values means they work together as a system — not in sequence, not independently, and not selectively. Think of it like the human body: you cannot say the circulatory system is more important than the respiratory system. Each Core Value strengthens the others, and neglecting one weakens the whole framework.

variants.stem: A ministry leader is strong in Accountability and Empowerment but neglects Balance entirely. According to the Core Values framework's emphasis on interconnection, what is the most likely result?
options: The leader's effectiveness in Accountability and Empowerment will be undermined because the values function as a mutually reinforcing system
The leader will still be highly effective because strength in two values compensates for weakness in a third
Only the leader's personal health will suffer; ministry outcomes will remain strong
The leader needs to abandon Accountability temporarily to develop Balance first

feedback.correct: Because the Core Values are interconnected, neglecting Balance will eventually erode the leader's capacity for Accountability (burnout makes truth-telling harder) and Empowerment (exhausted leaders hoard tasks rather than developing others). The system is only as strong as its weakest link.
feedback.incorrect: The Core Values are described as an interconnected system where each value amplifies the others. This means weakness in one area — like Balance — does not stay contained. It spreads and undermines the leader's capacity in the other values as well. Strength in some values cannot fully compensate for neglect of others.

questions.stem: In Week 1, the Personal Assessment Model identifies three inputs that produce authentic leadership. Which combination correctly represents those three inputs?
options: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change
Vision, Character, and Competence
Calling, Courage, and Community
Purpose, Passion, and Perseverance

feedback.correct: The Personal Assessment Model from Session 1 identifies Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change as the three inputs that produce the outputs of Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. This model established the foundation for honest self-assessment that the Core Values now build upon.
feedback.incorrect: The three inputs in the Personal Assessment Model are Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change. These produce the outcomes of Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. While terms like Vision, Competence, Courage, and Passion sound compelling, they are not the specific components of this model from Session 1.

variants.stem: According to the Personal Assessment Model from Week 1, the three inputs of Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change produce which set of outcomes?
options: Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability
Accountability, Balance, and Interdependence
Courage, Integrity, and Wisdom
Self-awareness, Empathy, and Resilience

feedback.correct: The Personal Assessment Model produces Authenticity (from Calling), Responsiveness (from Character), and Teachability (from Commitment to Change). Notice how Teachability connects directly to the Agenda for Change framework in Session 5 — both require willingness to face truth and pursue growth.
feedback.incorrect: The outputs of the Personal Assessment Model are Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability — not the Core Values (which come from Session 5) or generic leadership virtues. These three outcomes describe what a leader looks like when Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change are aligned.

questions.stem: In the Leadership Tree model from Week 4, which part of the tree represents a leader's support network — the people who sustain and stabilize the leader?
options: The Trunk
The Roots
The Branches and Leaves
The Fruit (Harvest)
justifications: The trunk provides structural support and stability for the entire tree, just as a leader's support network of mentors, peers, and allies provides the relational infrastructure that sustains leadership over time.
The trunk is the most visible part of the tree, and a support network is the most publicly visible aspect of a leader's life.
The trunk connects the roots to the branches, meaning the support network serves primarily as a communication channel between purpose and skills.
The trunk is chosen because it is the thickest part of the tree, symbolizing that the support network should be the largest category in a leader's life.

feedback.correct: The Trunk represents the support network because, like a tree's trunk, it provides the structural stability and sustenance that allows everything else to grow. Roots represent purpose (the hidden foundation), Branches and Leaves represent behavioral styles (DISC) and leadership skills (LSA), and the Harvest represents the fruit of integrated leadership.
feedback.incorrect: In the Leadership Tree: Roots = purpose (the deep, hidden foundation), Trunk = support network (structural stability), Branches/Leaves = DISC behavioral styles and LSA leadership skills (the visible expressions). The support network is specifically mapped to the trunk because of its stabilizing, sustaining function.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students correctly identify the trunk but for the wrong reason. The trunk's significance is not about visibility or size — it's about structural support and stability. Just as a trunk holds the tree upright through storms, a leader's support network provides the relational infrastructure that sustains them through challenges.

variants.stem: In the Leadership Tree metaphor from Week 4, a leader's DISC profile and Leadership Skills Assessment results are represented by which part of the tree?
